texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium: gas of awe, wonder, and worry. Is it time to give this noble gas the respect it deserves?
texts.text: Helium: gas of awe, wonder, and worry. Is it time to give this noble gas the respect it deserves?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium: such an inspiring gas to meet. The second most common element in the universe, the stuff of stars, is less dense than air, so if you lose your grip on a helium balloon, it rises into the air.  Breathing a mouthful of helium can make you sound like Donald Duck, and the reason lies in vocal folds and the speed of sound in helium. However, don't try this at home: the risk assessment includes potential death. [1]
texts.text: Helium: such an inspiring gas to meet. The second most common element in the universe, the stuff of stars, is less dense than air, so if you lose your grip on a helium balloon, it rises into the air.  Breathing a mouthful of helium can make you sound like Donald Duck, and the reason lies in vocal folds and the speed of sound in helium. However, don't try this at home: the risk assessment includes potential death. [1]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What do we know about helium? Where do we get it from? What do we use it for? What of our future relationship with this unreactive and fascinating gas?
texts.text: What do we know about helium? Where do we get it from? What do we use it for? What of our future relationship with this unreactive and fascinating gas?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Discovery
texts.text: Discovery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The word helium comes from Helios, Greek for the Sun, because this element was actually discovered in the Sun before it was known on Earth.  The spectrum of the Sun's corona, the 'crown' of hot gas surrounding the Sun's glowing surface, was analysed during a solar eclipse in 1868.
texts.text: The word helium comes from Helios, Greek for the Sun, because this element was actually discovered in the Sun before it was known on Earth.  The spectrum of the Sun's corona, the 'crown' of hot gas surrounding the Sun's glowing surface, was analysed during a solar eclipse in 1868.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different elements absorb different colours of light, giving a 'bar code' for the different elements present. In addition to the bar codes for known elements like hydrogen and sodium,
texts.text: Different elements absorb different colours of light, giving a 'bar code' for the different elements present. In addition to the bar codes for known elements like hydrogen and sodium,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Sun's spectrum, showing the characteristic bar codes caused by different elements absorbing photons (light) of specific energies.
texts.text: The Sun's spectrum, showing the characteristic bar codes caused by different elements absorbing photons (light) of specific energies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Public domain
texts.text: Public domain

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jules Janssen from France found a set of lines that didn't fit with any known element, and Norman Lockyer from England suggested that the Sun contained an element unknown on Earth and gave it the name helium.
texts.text: Jules Janssen from France found a set of lines that didn't fit with any known element, and Norman Lockyer from England suggested that the Sun contained an element unknown on Earth and gave it the name helium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 1881 the same pattern was discovered in gas from Mount Vesuvius  in  Italy.  Eventually,  helium  was  collected:    a  very low-density, unreactive gas to join argon, xenon, and krypton, and in 1902, Mendeleev added a new column to his periodic table for these 'noble gases'.
texts.text: In 1881 the same pattern was discovered in gas from Mount Vesuvius  in  Italy.  Eventually,  helium  was  collected:    a  very low-density, unreactive gas to join argon, xenon, and krypton, and in 1902, Mendeleev added a new column to his periodic table for these 'noble gases'.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: An image of the Sun's corona taken during the total solar eclipse of 21 August 2017.  The white glow of the corona is sculpted by the Sun's magnetic field.
texts.text: An image of the Sun's corona taken during the total solar eclipse of 21 August 2017.  The white glow of the corona is sculpted by the Sun's magnetic field.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Where do we get our helium from?
texts.text: Where do we get our helium from?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium  is  the  second  most  common  element  in  the  Universe; it  was formed during the Big Bang and there is lots in stars, which release energy by fusing hydrogen atoms to give helium. However, Earth's helium is produced by alpha decay within the Earth's crust. Radioactive elements, such as isotopes of uranium, emit alpha particles, which are helium nuclei. These doubly positively charged alpha particles pick up a couple of electrons and there you are: a helium atom.
texts.text: Helium  is  the  second  most  common  element  in  the  Universe; it  was formed during the Big Bang and there is lots in stars, which release energy by fusing hydrogen atoms to give helium. However, Earth's helium is produced by alpha decay within the Earth's crust. Radioactive elements, such as isotopes of uranium, emit alpha particles, which are helium nuclei. These doubly positively charged alpha particles pick up a couple of electrons and there you are: a helium atom.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The very small helium atoms find their way through gaps between particles in permeable rocks, so helium leaks out of the  Earth  over  millions  of  years.  There  are  just  a  few  rare wells of helium gas, which form when pockets of helium are trapped by impermeable rock.
texts.text: The very small helium atoms find their way through gaps between particles in permeable rocks, so helium leaks out of the  Earth  over  millions  of  years.  There  are  just  a  few  rare wells of helium gas, which form when pockets of helium are trapped by impermeable rock.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Examples can be found in Qatar, the USA, Russia, and Algeria. Some of these are becoming depleted, and the geopolitics of others makes supply unreliable. The largest well is estimated to about seven times the annual global consumption, [2] and the estimated total US known reserves might supply twenty years. However, helium is an element, so we can't synthesise more of it, and we have used millions, or even billions, of years' accumulation of this precious element in just a few decades.
texts.text: Examples can be found in Qatar, the USA, Russia, and Algeria. Some of these are becoming depleted, and the geopolitics of others makes supply unreliable. The largest well is estimated to about seven times the annual global consumption, [2] and the estimated total US known reserves might supply twenty years. However, helium is an element, so we can't synthesise more of it, and we have used millions, or even billions, of years' accumulation of this precious element in just a few decades.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium  is  unique  amongst  the  light  elements  in  that  it  is a  product  of  the  Earth,  rather  than  the  star  debris  that preceded it. All of Earth's helium has been made during its own history.
texts.text: Helium  is  unique  amongst  the  light  elements  in  that  it  is a  product  of  the  Earth,  rather  than  the  star  debris  that preceded it. All of Earth's helium has been made during its own history.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Where does helium go?
texts.text: Where does helium go?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why have billions of years of helium release from rocks not given  Earth  a  helium  atmosphere?  Helium  atoms  are  lowmass, and so they are fast-moving particles in air. Any helium atoms high enough and fast enough can escape gravity's pull: Earth leaks helium to space.
texts.text: Why have billions of years of helium release from rocks not given  Earth  a  helium  atmosphere?  Helium  atoms  are  lowmass, and so they are fast-moving particles in air. Any helium atoms high enough and fast enough can escape gravity's pull: Earth leaks helium to space.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What makes helium so useful?
texts.text: What makes helium so useful?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The biggest application of helium is in cryogenics. Liquid helium is a 'magic ticket' towards absolute zero - the lowest temperature possible. Helium condenses at -269oC (4 K), and at  this  temperature, most metals become superconductors and  let  an  electric  current  flow  with  zero  resistance.  This means that helium is key wherever very strong electromagnets are needed.
texts.text: The biggest application of helium is in cryogenics. Liquid helium is a 'magic ticket' towards absolute zero - the lowest temperature possible. Helium condenses at -269oC (4 K), and at  this  temperature, most metals become superconductors and  let  an  electric  current  flow  with  zero  resistance.  This means that helium is key wherever very strong electromagnets are needed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An  example  is  magnetic  resonance  imaging  (MRI)  scanners. They make details like torn ligaments, tumours, and heart  defects  visible  without  surgery,  which  has  transformed medical diagnosis. They account for about 30-35% of the helium used each year globally, [3,4]  but they remain rare in many parts of the world, so this demand should grow. Do you know anyone who has had an MRI scan, possibly a lifesaving one? This wouldn't be possible without helium.
texts.text: An  example  is  magnetic  resonance  imaging  (MRI)  scanners. They make details like torn ligaments, tumours, and heart  defects  visible  without  surgery,  which  has  transformed medical diagnosis. They account for about 30-35% of the helium used each year globally, [3,4]  but they remain rare in many parts of the world, so this demand should grow. Do you know anyone who has had an MRI scan, possibly a lifesaving one? This wouldn't be possible without helium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In scientific research, we know about the structures of proteins and other complex molecules thanks to nuclear magnetic  resonance  (NMR)  spectrometers,  the  cousins  of  MRI. Particle accelerators such as those at CERN, the birthplace of the world-wide-web and the site of the hunt for the Higgs Boson, also depend on vast electromagnets cooled by helium to steer charged particles.
texts.text: In scientific research, we know about the structures of proteins and other complex molecules thanks to nuclear magnetic  resonance  (NMR)  spectrometers,  the  cousins  of  MRI. Particle accelerators such as those at CERN, the birthplace of the world-wide-web and the site of the hunt for the Higgs Boson, also depend on vast electromagnets cooled by helium to steer charged particles.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: A medical MRI Scanner Jan Ainali/Wikipedia, CC-BY-3.0
texts.text: A medical MRI Scanner Jan Ainali/Wikipedia, CC-BY-3.0

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a noble gas, helium is also inert, which makes it useful in applications where a non-reactive atmosphere is needed, for example, in certain kinds of welding and to purge production lines of other more reactive substances. Semiconductor manufacture for electronics relies on helium because of its inert nature and fluid properties. [5]
texts.text: As a noble gas, helium is also inert, which makes it useful in applications where a non-reactive atmosphere is needed, for example, in certain kinds of welding and to purge production lines of other more reactive substances. Semiconductor manufacture for electronics relies on helium because of its inert nature and fluid properties. [5]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium's inertness also means that it is non-toxic. Deep-sea divers use helium in their breathing mix to reduce the nitrogen absorbed into the blood during deep dives. Nitrogen dissolved in the blood can form bubbles when the diver surfaces, leading to 'the bends', which is painful and can be deadly.
texts.text: Helium's inertness also means that it is non-toxic. Deep-sea divers use helium in their breathing mix to reduce the nitrogen absorbed into the blood during deep dives. Nitrogen dissolved in the blood can form bubbles when the diver surfaces, leading to 'the bends', which is painful and can be deadly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally,  the  small  size  of  helium  atoms  means  that  helium gas can find its way through gaps on the atomic scale, which makes it useful in research and industry to check for leaks.
texts.text: Finally,  the  small  size  of  helium  atoms  means  that  helium gas can find its way through gaps on the atomic scale, which makes it useful in research and industry to check for leaks.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Helium: an endangered element?
texts.text: Helium: an endangered element?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So, we have a problem. Modern life depends on the use of an element that is not only finite but leaks away from the Earth, and whose usage is quickly outstripping reserves. Radioac- tive decay in the Earth is producing more as you read this article, but at a rate that is tiny compared to the rate of use.
texts.text: So, we have a problem. Modern life depends on the use of an element that is not only finite but leaks away from the Earth, and whose usage is quickly outstripping reserves. Radioac- tive decay in the Earth is producing more as you read this article, but at a rate that is tiny compared to the rate of use.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium is a key element in our lives, but how common is this  knowledge? Does it need a better press along with the  scientific  campaign [6] for  its  conservation,  recycling, and reuse?
texts.text: Helium is a key element in our lives, but how common is this  knowledge? Does it need a better press along with the  scientific  campaign [6] for  its  conservation,  recycling, and reuse?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Meanwhile, 17% of our global consumption [4] is  used to fill balloons. A precious and important gas is lost to space and the balloons fall as litter, endangering wildlife. [7] Should we rethink the ethics of this?  Are party balloons really worth serious helium shortages for future generations? [8]
texts.text: Meanwhile, 17% of our global consumption [4] is  used to fill balloons. A precious and important gas is lost to space and the balloons fall as litter, endangering wildlife. [7] Should we rethink the ethics of this?  Are party balloons really worth serious helium shortages for future generations? [8]

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: AUTHOR BIOGRAPHY
texts.text: AUTHOR BIOGRAPHY

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Melissa Lord was a physics teacher for many years, and was head of science at Altrincham Girls' Grammar School near Manchester. She is a Regional Representative for the Ogden Trust, a UK charity promoting the teaching and learning of physics, and also leads the New Teacher Subject Days in physics for the PTI, a charity providing training to develop teachers' subject knowledge. She is a Trustee at Catalyst Science Discovery Centre and Museum in Widnes, Cheshire, and volunteers with the Sustainability Physics for Schools project.
texts.text: Melissa Lord was a physics teacher for many years, and was head of science at Altrincham Girls' Grammar School near Manchester. She is a Regional Representative for the Ogden Trust, a UK charity promoting the teaching and learning of physics, and also leads the New Teacher Subject Days in physics for the PTI, a charity providing training to develop teachers' subject knowledge. She is a Trustee at Catalyst Science Discovery Centre and Museum in Widnes, Cheshire, and volunteers with the Sustainability Physics for Schools project.
